channel.title: DFIR Radar
channel.description: Keeping DFIR Intelligence on your Radar. The best DFIR research from hundreds of sources, filtered for relevance.

item.title: Qilin EDR killer infection chain
item.description:
Qilin ransomware deploys sophisticated multi-stage EDR killer targeting 300+ security products. Advanced loader uses SEH/VEH-based obfuscation and kernel manipulation to completely disable endpoint detection systems.
Technical breakdown: • Stage 1: Malicious msimg32.dll side-loaded via DLL hijacking, implements slot-policy table for syscall evasion and Halo's Gate technique • Stage 2-3: Complex VEH-based control flow obfuscation, overwrites ExitProcess IAT entry, maps payload into shell32.dll memory space • Stage 4: EDR killer loads rwdrv.sys (abused ThrottleStop driver) for physical memory R/W, hlpdrv.sys for process termination (T1562.001) • Kernel manipulation: Unregisters EDR callbacks for process/thread/image events, overwrites CiValidateImageHeader with ArbPreprocessEntry • Geo-fencing excludes post-Soviet countries, requires admin privileges for driver loading
Attack chain systematically blinds behavioral detection before ransomware deployment. Hunt for msimg32.dll side-loading, unsigned drivers in system directories, and unexpected ExitProcess IAT modifications.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039644788887318919

item.title: Inside CVE-2024-4367: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution via PDF.js
item.description:
CVE-2024-4367 (CVSS 8.8) in PDF.js allows arbitrary JavaScript execution via malicious FontMatrix values in PDFs. Still widely exploitable in 2026 due to AI-generated code using vulnerable versions. Upgrade to 4.2.67+ or disable isEvalSupported.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039629571146776816

item.title: Axios Just Got Weaponized — And Your npm install Pulled the Trigger
item.description:
Supply chain attack compromises axios npm package via malicious dependency injection. Attackers gained maintainer credentials to push trojanized versions that executed cross-platform RATs during installation.
Key technical details: • Malicious axios@1.14.1 and @0.30.4 added plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency with postinstall script executing node setup.js automatically • Multi-stage payload drops platform-specific RATs: Windows uses PowerShell + VBScript, Linux deploys Python script to /tmp/ld.py, macOS drops Mach-O binary to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com:8000/6202033 with HTTP POST beacons using User-Agent "mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0)" • Windows persistence via HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\MicrosoftUpdate registry key • Self-deletion after execution: fs.unlink(__filename) removes loader, renames package.md to package.json to appear legitimate
Hunt for recent axios installations, monitor %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, /tmp/ld.py, and /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond file creation. Check registry Run keys for MicrosoftUpdate entries and network connections to sfrclak[.]com.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039614585079001308

item.title: Hooked on Linux: Rootkit Detection Engineering
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs exposes fundamental flaws in static rootkit detection, showing how trivial binary modifications evade traditional signatures. Research introduces comprehensive behavioral detection framework for Linux rootkits across userland, kernel, eBPF, and io_uring attack vectors.
Key findings: • Static detection fails catastrophically: single null byte addition drops detection rates from 36/66 to 21/66 engines for Azazel rootkit • Behavioral indicators provide reliable detection: LD_PRELOAD abuse, shared object creation in /tmp/.hidden/, LKM loading via init_module/finit_module syscalls • Emerging attack vectors require new telemetry: eBPF rootkits using bpf_probe_write_user helper, io_uring-based stealth techniques bypassing syscall monitoring • Persistence mechanisms span multiple layers: shell config modification, udev rule abuse (/etc/udev/rules.d/), kernel module autoload via /etc/modules-load.d/ • Defense evasion includes process masquerading as kworker/kthreadd, timestomping via touch command, log clearing through dmesg -c
Hunt for unusual shared objects in ephemeral paths, monitor high-numbered kill signals (32+), and implement Auditd rules for init_module/finit_module syscalls. Full detection rules and YARA signatures available in Elastic's detection-rules repository.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039539284336591359

item.title: Anthropic Claude Code Leak
item.description:
Anthropic Claude Code source leak weaponized: Threat actors distribute Vidar stealer and GhostSocks proxy malware through fake GitHub repositories masquerading as the leaked AI agent codebase.
Key technical details: • Original leak: 59.8MB JavaScript source map exposed 513K lines of TypeScript code via npm package @anthropic-ai/claude-code v2.1.88 • Malicious repos rank high in Google searches for "leaked Claude Code" • Dropper: ClaudeCode_x64.exe (Rust-based) delivers Vidar v18.7 + GhostSocks proxy tools • IOCs: Archive hashes d8256fbc62e85dae85eb8d4b49613774, C2s steamcommunity[.]com/profiles/76561198721263282 and 147.45.197[.]92:443 • Attack leverages supply chain confusion - thousands of GitHub forks create perfect cover for trojanized versions
DFIR impact: • Exposed agent hooks, MCP integrations, OAuth flows enable precise exploitation of legitimate Claude deployments • Perfect timing overlap with malicious Axios npm supply chain attack (March 31) • Developer workstation compromise via malicious dependencies in fake source builds
Hunt for recent GitHub clones of "leaked-claude-code" repos, unusual Rust executables, and outbound connections to Steam community profiles from developer workstations.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039523871116628135

item.title: How we caught the Axios supply chain attack
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs catches massive Axios supply chain attack using AI-powered package monitoring tool. DPRK-attributed actors compromised npm maintainer accounts, injecting cross-platform malware via phantom dependency affecting one of npm's most popular packages.
Key technical details: • Attackers compromised maintainer accounts, published malicious Axios v1.14.1 and v0.30.4 with phantom dependency "plain-crypto-js" • Malware deployed via postinstall hooks, featuring cross-platform RAT with steganography in WAV files • Part of larger TeamPCP campaign that previously hit Trivy, LiteLLM, and Telnyx packages using stolen CI/CD credentials • Detection via real-time diff analysis of package changes using Cursor AI agent with custom prompts
Attack chain: • Compromised npm accounts → changed email to ProtonMail → published malicious versions • Added phantom dependency instead of direct code injection to evade detection • Postinstall scripts deployed persistent malware disguised as msbuild.exe on Windows • C2 server overwhelmed by infection volume, limiting impact scope
DFIR artifacts: • Elastic Defend alerts on infected hosts provided initial detection signals • Package installation logs showing phantom dependency downloads • Postinstall script execution evidence in process telemetry
Implement package soak time with `npm config set min-release-age 7` to delay updates. Full technical analysis and detection rules available from Elastic Security Labs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039508768011309187

item.title: Mitigating the Axios npm supply chain compromise
item.description:
North Korean 🇰🇵 Sapphire Sleet compromises Axios npm packages with malicious dependency injection. Two versions (1.14.1, 0.30.4) deployed cross-platform RATs targeting 70M+ weekly downloads on March 31, 2026.
Attack mechanics: • Malicious plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency executes automatically via post-install hook (T1195.002) • C2 domain sfrclak[.]com:8000 serves OS-specific payloads via single endpoint • Windows: PowerShell RAT with registry persistence (HKCU Run key), drops %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe • macOS: Native binary /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond masquerades as Apple component • Linux: Python payload /tmp/ld.py launched detached via nohup
DFIR artifacts: • Registry: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\MicrosoftUpdate • File paths: %TEMP%\6202033.vbs, %PROGRAMDATA%\system.bat, /tmp/ld.py • Network: POST requests to packages.npm.org/product[0-2] as OS identifiers • Process chains: npm install → node setup.js → platform-specific stagers
Hunt for npm post-install processes spawning curl/PowerShell with network connections to port 8000, and search node_modules for plain-crypto-js packages. Full IOC list and hunting queries in the Microsoft report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039493773718077787

item.title: Prioritizing Alerts Triage with Higher-Order Detection Rules
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs reveals Higher-Order Rules methodology for correlating multiple alerts across endpoints, network, and observability data. Production testing shows 65 atomic rules generate 8,000 daily alerts, reduced to ~30 prioritized correlations.
Key technical implementation: • ES|QL queries correlate by shared entities (host.id, user, IP, process.entity_id) with temporal logic and prevalence filtering • Cross-domain detection combines endpoint + network (PANW/FortiGate), endpoint + email, and security + observability metrics • "Newly observed" rules surface first-time alerts within 5-7 day lookback windows using DATE_DIFF functions • Process tree correlation via process.Ext.ancestry field identifies related malicious activity across process chains
Detection patterns: • Entity-based correlation: Multiple Elastic Defend alerts (≥3 unique rules OR ≥2 event codes) on same host in 24h window • Network enrichment: JOIN endpoint alerts with firewall C2 detections using source.ip = host.ip correlation • Observability fusion: CPU spike metrics + memory signature alerts = high-confidence cryptominer detection • Lateral movement: Alert correlation where alert_1.host.ip = alert_2.source.ip
Hunt for multiple distinct security alerts converging on the same host, user, or process within short timeframes. Full ES|QL correlation queries and 30+ detection rules available in the research.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039478482908971081

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack by North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor UNC1069
item.description:
North Korea 🇰🇵-nexus UNC1069 compromised the axios npm package (100M+ weekly downloads) for three hours on March 31, delivering WAVESHAPER.V2 cross-platform RAT to potentially millions of developer environments worldwide.
Attack breakdown: • Compromised @jasonsaayman maintainer account, changed email to ifstap@proton.me, used long-lived npm token to bypass legitimate GitHub Actions workflow • Injected malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" with postinstall hook executing SILKBELL dropper (reversed Base64 + XOR cipher, key: "OrDeR_7077") • WAVESHAPER.V2 deployed platform-specific payloads: macOS binary to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond, Windows PowerShell to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, Linux Python to /tmp/ld.py • C2 beacons every 60 seconds to sfrclak[.]com:8000 using Base64 JSON with IE8/XP User-Agent spoofing • Anti-forensic cleanup: deletes dropper, replaces package.json with clean stub post-infection
StepSecurity detected C2 contact in 12,000+ projects. Hunt for hardcoded artifacts by platform and block C2 domain. Full IOCs, YARA rules, and detection rules available in GTIG report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039463565472788947

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions About the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack by North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor UNC1069
item.description:
North Korea 🇰🇵-nexus UNC1069 compromised axios npm package (100M+ weekly downloads) for 3 hours on March 31, delivering WAVESHAPER.V2 cross-platform RAT. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 deployed backdoors to potentially hundreds of thousands of developer systems.
Key technical details: • Attacker compromised @jasonsaayman npm account, bypassed GitHub Actions OIDC via long-lived classic token • Malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" uses postinstall hook executing SILKBELL dropper with dual encoding (reversed Base64 + XOR key "OrDeR_7077") • WAVESHAPER.V2 payloads: macOS binary (/Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond), Windows PowerShell (%PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe), Linux Python (/tmp/ld.py) • C2 beacons every 60s to sfrclak[.]com:8000 using Base64 JSON, supports kill/rundir/runscript/peinject commands • Anti-forensic cleanup deletes dropper, restores clean package.json from stub file
DFIR artifacts: • Registry persistence: HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run "MicrosoftUpdate" • Hardcoded User-Agent: Internet Explorer 8 on Windows XP • Socket.dev detected compromise within 6 minutes, full IOC list and YARA rules available from GTIG
Hunt for axios installations between March 31 00:21-03:15 UTC and network connections to C2 infrastructure. Full IOC list and detection rules in GTIG report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039448378392666359

item.title: CVE-2025-53521: F5 BIG-IP APM Flaw Reclassified as Unauthenticated RCE
item.description:
CVE-2025-53521: Critical F5 BIG-IP APM vulnerability reclassified from DoS to unauthenticated RCE (CVSS 9.8). Initially dismissed as service disruption, now confirmed as pre-auth code execution with active exploitation.
Key technical details: • Affects BIG-IP APM when access policy configured on virtual server • Vulnerable versions: 15.1.0-15.1.10, 16.1.0-16.1.6, 17.1.0-17.1.2, 17.5.0-17.5.1 • Exploitation via crafted traffic to APM-enabled virtual servers • CISA KEV-listed with confirmed active exploitation campaigns • Memory-only webshell behavior complicates artifact detection
IOCs to hunt: • Suspicious files: /run/bigtlog.pipe, /run/bigstart.ltm • Modified system binaries: /usr/bin/umount, /usr/sbin/httpd • iControl REST activity from localhost • HTTP 201 responses with CSS content-type camouflage • SELinux disable attempts
Impact assessment required: • Priority: Internet-facing virtual servers with APM policies • Cross-reference process behavior, config changes, audit logs • Consider rebuild over cleanup if compromise window unclear
Hunt for unusual /mgmt/shared/identified-devices/config/device-info access and correlate multiple indicators since file-based IOCs may be absent.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039312596826755394

item.title: North Korea’s Lazarus Group Behind the Axios npm Supply Chain Attack
item.description:
North Korea's 🇰🇵 Lazarus Group compromised the axios JavaScript library—downloaded 100M+ times weekly—injecting WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor via supply chain attack. Three-hour exposure window on March 31 affected 80% of cloud environments with potential for massive credential theft and downstream attacks.
Key technical details: • Compromised maintainer account inserted malicious "plain-crypto-js" dependency into axios v1.14.1 and v0.30.4 • SILKBELL dropper uses postinstall hooks for silent execution, deploys platform-specific payloads • WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor connects to sfrclak[.]com:8000/6202033 using anomalous IE8/WinXP user-agent string • Payloads drop to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe (Windows), /tmp/ld.py (Linux) • Anti-forensic cleanup deletes dropper and reverts package.json modifications
Impact assessment: • 3% execution rate in affected environments during 3-hour window • Hundreds of thousands of stolen credentials enable future supply chain attacks, ransomware, crypto theft • Downstream risk to dependent packages extends blast radius significantly
Check systems for RAT artifacts at specified paths, audit CI/CD logs for March 31 00:21-03:20 UTC, rotate credentials where artifacts found, block sfrclak[.]com egress.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039267140730073386

item.description:
TeamPCP's CanisterWorm malware harvests npm tokens and autonomously spreads across packages, converting software registries into propagation infrastructure. Self-replicating supply chain attacks now bypass traditional vulnerability-based defenses.

item.title: A laughing RAT: CrystalX combines spyware, stealer, and prankware features
item.description:
Kaspersky discovers CrystalX RAT - a unique MaaS combining traditional spyware with "prankware" features designed to troll victims. Active since January 2026, this Go-based malware targets credentials, cryptocurrency, and includes anti-analysis evasion.
Technical breakdown: • Encryption: zlib compression + ChaCha20 with hardcoded 32-byte key and 12-byte nonce • Anti-analysis: VM detection, MITM checks, debug loops, and stealth patches for AMSI/ETW bypass • Stealer: Extracts Steam/Discord/Telegram credentials, uses ChromeElevator utility dropped to %TEMP%\svc[rndInt].exe • Clipper: Injects malicious extension into Chrome/Edge via %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Edge\ExtSvc directory • Unique "Rofl" panel: Desktop rotation, mouse remapping, cursor shake, BSoD simulation, peripheral disruption
C2 communication via WebSocket protocol sends data in JSON plaintext. VNC functionality enables remote desktop control with input blocking capabilities.
Hunt for suspicious files in %TEMP% matching pattern svc[digits].exe and monitor Chrome DevTools CDP protocol usage for unauthorized extension loading.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039221850283065379

item.title: Axios npm package compromised to deploy malware
item.description:
Supply chain attack compromises popular Axios npm package. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 deployed cross-platform RAT after maintainer account takeover, impacting macOS, Windows, and Linux systems globally.
Technical details: • Compromised packages: axios-1.14.1.tgz, axios-0.30.4.tgz, and dependency plain-crypto-js-4.2.1.tgz • Multi-stage payload chain: setup.js → platform-specific RATs (system.bat for Windows, ld.py for Linux, com.apple.act.mond for macOS) • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com:8000 (142[.]11[.]206[.]73) with platform-specific endpoints • Anti-forensics: Removes installation artifacts and replaces package metadata post-deployment • Windows persistence via C:\ProgramData\wt.exe and system.bat batch script
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor npm install logs and package-lock.json for affected versions • Hunt for executables in C:\ProgramData\wt.exe and suspicious batch files • Check network connections to sfrclak[.]com and callnrwise[.]com domains • Review PowerShell execution logs for obfuscated scripts (Sophos: Troj/PSAgent-CN)
Immediate actions: Audit all Axios installations, check SHA256 hashes against IOC list, and scan for C2 beaconing. Full IOC table with 16+ hashes available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039191602040852954

item.title: Axios Front-End Library npm Supply Chain Poisoning Alert
item.description:
Supply chain attack: Attackers compromised axios maintainer accounts, bypassing CI/CD to inject malware into versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Over 300M weekly downloads affected across all platforms.
Attack methodology: • Attackers hijacked Jason Saayman's npm/GitHub accounts, changed email to ifstap@proton.me • Created clean plain-crypto-js@4.2.0 package first, then malicious @4.2.1 version 18hrs later to evade detection • Bypassed normal GitHub Actions CI/CD, manually released poisoned axios versions via npm CLI • RAT dropper (setup.js) detects OS and deploys platform-specific payloads to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), /tmp/ld.py (Linux), %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe (Windows) • Self-destructs after execution: deletes setup.js, replaces package.json with clean version
DFIR artifacts: • Malicious versions: axios@1.14.1 (SHA1: 2553649f2322049666871cea80a5d0d6adc700ca), axios@0.30.4 (SHA1: d6f3f62fd3b9f5432f5782b62d8cfd5247d5ee71) • C2 domains: sfrclak.com, callnrwise.com (142.11.206.73) • User-Agent: mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0; windows nt 5.1; trident/4.0)
Check npm dependencies for affected versions and hunt for platform-specific backdoor files at the locations above. Full IOCs and detection commands in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039176751260090479

item.title: Hackers compromise Axios npm package to drop cross-platform malware
item.description:
Major supply chain attack: Hackers compromised Axios npm package (400M monthly downloads) to deliver cross-platform RATs. North Korean 🇰🇵 UNC1069 suspected behind the sophisticated attack targeting developers worldwide.
Key technical details: • Malicious versions axios@1.14.1 and axios@0.30.4 injected plain-crypto-js@^4.2.1 dependency • Post-install script (setup.js) contacted C2 sfrclak.com for OS-specific payloads • Windows: PowerShell copied to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe for persistence (T1036.003) • macOS: Binary dropped to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond via AppleScript • Linux: Python payload at /tmp/ld.py executed with nohup • Self-destruction mechanism replaced package.json with clean copy post-infection
Attack methodology: • Compromised maintainer Jason Saayman's npm and GitHub accounts • Email changed to ifstap@proton.me, removed compromise reports • 18-hour advance staging indicates planned operation, not opportunistic • RAT capabilities: command execution, file enumeration, base64 binary deployment
DFIR artifacts: Check for plain-crypto-js presence, monitor %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond, /tmp/ld.py. Rotate all credentials on affected systems - potential data exfiltration includes API keys and tokens.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039161507603419270

item.title: Supply chain attack on Axios npm package: Scope, impact, and remediations
item.description:
Supply chain attack compromises Axios npm package with 100M+ weekly downloads. Attackers hijacked maintainer account, injected RAT via malicious dependency "plain-crypto-js" in versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Immediate containment required.
Attack timeline and methodology: • March 31, 2026 1AM UTC: Compromised maintainer published malicious Axios versions • Injected "plain-crypto-js@4.2.1" dependency with postinstall hook dropper (T1195.002) • Double-obfuscated setup.js (SHA256: e10b1fa84f1d6481625f741b69892780140d4e0e7769e7491e5f4d894c2e0e09) • Cross-platform RAT deployment targeting MacOS, Windows, Linux via C2 sfrclak[.]com:8000
DFIR artifacts and detection: • Package.json/lockfiles containing Axios 1.14.1 or 0.30.4 indicate full compromise • Monitor npm postinstall executions and outbound connections to sfrclak[.]com • Check filesystem for plain-crypto-js presence across development environments • Network logs showing C2 communication patterns from Node.js processes
Critical window: Attackers typically abuse stolen credentials within hours. Treat any detection as active breach requiring immediate quarantine, credential rotation, and full IR activation.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039146354887278597

item.title: Reduce conn.log from 35GB to 5GB with a Simple Hook
item.description:
Simple Zeek log filtering hook eliminates 85% of conn.log noise by blocking redundant scanner connection attempts. NERSC reduced daily logs from 35GB to 5GB using 15 lines of code targeting S0 connection states from known scanners.
Technical implementation: • Hook intercepts Conn::log_policy before writing to conn.log • Filters incomplete connections (S, Sr, SW history) from Scan::known_scanners set • Preserves all completed connections (full TCP handshakes with data exchange) • Requires bro-simple-scan or equivalent to populate known_scanners set • Works best with dual-sided network edge monitoring for blocked traffic visibility
DFIR benefits: • Dramatically improves signal-to-noise ratio for threat hunting • Reduces storage overhead and search time during investigations • Maintains complete logging for legitimate connection attempts • Eliminates redundant entries from already-blocked malicious hosts
Apply this pattern to your environment: identify your noisiest log sources, determine what traffic you've already categorized as low-value, then write targeted hooks to filter it. Full code sample available in the original Zeek community discussion.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039131235914547706

item.title: North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor Compromises Widely Used Axios NPM Package in Supply Chain Attack
item.description:
Active supply chain attack: North Korea 🇰🇵-nexus UNC1069 compromised widely-used Axios NPM package, inserting WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor into versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Over 180 million weekly downloads potentially affected.
Attack timeline: • March 31, 2026 00:21-03:20 UTC: Attacker compromised maintainer account, changed email to ifstap@proton.me • Malicious "plain-crypto-js" dependency added with postinstall hook executing setup.js dropper • SILKBELL dropper (SHA256: e10b1fa84f1d6481625f741b69892780140d4e0e7769e7491e5f4d894c2e0e09) delivers OS-specific payloads
Platform-specific execution: • Windows: Copies powershell.exe to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, downloads PS script to %TEMP%\6202033.ps1 • macOS: Downloads Mach-O binary to /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond via curl POST • Linux: Deploys Python backdoor to /tmp/ld.py • C2: sfrclak[.]com:8000 (142.11.206.73) with 60-second beacons using hardcoded IE8 User-Agent
WAVESHAPER.V2 capabilities include reconnaissance, PE injection, shell execution, and file enumeration. Windows persistence via HKCU Run key "MicrosoftUpdate" launching %PROGRAMDATA%\system.bat.
Hunt for Node.js processes spawning PowerShell with hidden flags, curl POST requests to low-reputation domains, and the provided YARA signatures. Full IOC list and detection rules available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039116279219413160

item.title: Axios supply chain attack chops away at npm trust
item.description:
Supply chain attack compromises Axios npm packages with RAT payload. Attackers used maintainer credentials to publish malicious versions reaching 100M weekly downloads, targeting developer environments with credential-stealing malware.
Technical details: • Malicious packages: axios@1.14.1, axios@0.30.4, and dependency plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 • Postinstall script (setup.js) downloads platform-specific RAT for macOS/Windows/Linux (T1195.002) • C2 domain: sfrclak[.]com (142.11.206.73) - blocked by security vendors • Dropper self-destructs leaving no traces in node_modules inspection or npm audit
Attack chain: • Compromised maintainer credentials used to publish poisoned packages to npm • Auto-execution via postinstall hooks during npm install (T1072) • Targets developer secrets: cloud keys, deploy tokens, API credentials • Persistence files: /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), /tmp/ld.py (Linux), %PROGRAMDATA%\wt (Windows)
DFIR impact: • Any environment that installed affected versions should be treated as fully compromised • Rotate all secrets accessible to build/deploy pipelines immediately • Check for persistence files and C2 communications in developer workstations
Hunt for npm postinstall script executions with network connections to suspicious domains and check build environments for the listed file paths.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039101170975748436

item.title: Latest Xloader Obfuscation Methods and Network Protocol
item.description:
Zscaler's deep dive into Xloader v8.7 reveals sophisticated new obfuscation techniques that significantly complicate automated analysis and detection. The info-stealer now uses randomized parameter construction, enhanced code obfuscation, and 65 decoy C2 servers to mask operations.
Key technical details: • Enhanced function decryption: Parameters built out-of-order byte-by-byte instead of consistent patterns, breaking traditional automation tools • Multi-layer RC4 encryption with 5 different keys (PKT2, HTTP GET, C2 URL hash, derived seeds) plus Base64 encoding variations • Network protocol uses "XLNG" magic header, supports 9 command types including PowerShell/DLL execution, credential theft, system shutdown (T1529) • Decoy infrastructure: Randomly selects 16 from 65 encrypted C2 IPs per communication cycle to evade sandbox detection
DFIR artifacts: • HTTP traffic with modified Base64 chars (+ / = become - _ .) in POST requests • User-Agent varies: "Windows Explorer" (WinINet) vs browser mimicry (raw sockets) • Query parameters: random alphanumeric names with "&dat=" prefix and "&wn=1" flag
Hunt for HTTP POST requests with modified Base64 encoding patterns and multiple RC4-encrypted layers. Monitor for PowerShell execution following suspicious HTTP GET requests to multiple IPs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039086021091197066

item.title: Axios Hijacked: npm Account Takeover Deploys Cross-Platform RAT to Millions
item.description:
CRITICAL: Axios npm package compromised via account takeover, deploying cross-platform RAT to millions. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 were live for ~3 hours on March 31st before removal.
Attack breakdown: • Attacker hijacked maintainer's npm token, changed email to ProtonMail address, bypassed OIDC controls • Phantom dependency "plain-crypto-js@4.2.1" executes postinstall hook deploying platform-specific RATs • macOS: /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond | Windows: %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe | Linux: /tmp/ld.py • Self-destructing payload erases evidence within 1.1 seconds, contacts C2 sfrclak[.]com:8000 • RAT capabilities include remote execution, shell commands, filesystem enumeration with 60s beacons
DFIR artifacts: • Check lockfiles for axios@1.14.1 or 0.30.4, search for node_modules/plain-crypto-js/ directory • Hunt for RAT binaries at specified file paths across all platforms • Network connections to 142.11.206.73:8000 indicate active compromise • Timeline: exposure window 00:21-03:15 UTC March 31st, 2026
Immediate actions: Downgrade to safe versions (1.14.0/0.30.3), treat affected systems as fully compromised, rotate all accessible credentials. Full IOC list and payload hashes available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039070978689671269

item.title: Fake Installers to Monero: A Multi-Tool Mining Operation
item.description:
Multi-year cryptomining operation REF1695 generates $9,400+ from fake installers distributing RATs and custom XMRig loaders. Active since late 2023, operator monetizes through both mining and CPA fraud across four distinct campaign variants.
Key technical findings: • Consistent Themida/WinLicense + .NET Reactor packing across all campaigns • CNB Bot: Previously unreported .NET implant with RSA-2048 task authentication, 10-minute scheduled execution via VBScript wrapper • Custom XMRig loader kills mining process when 35+ analysis tools detected (Taskmgr, ProcessHacker, Wireshark, Procmon) • WinRing0x64.sys driver enables Ring 0 hardware access for MSR modification, boosting RandomX hash rates • Four active Monero wallets: 27.88 XMR paid out, 14 workers still active
Attack chain progression: • ISO containers with ReadMe.txt social engineering bypass SmartScreen warnings • PowerShell adds broad Defender exclusions (%TEMP%, %LocalAppData%, %AppData%, LOLBins) • Multi-stage .NET loaders deploy PureRAT v3.0.1, PureMiner, SilentCryptoMiner, AsyncRAT, PulsarRAT • Persistence via scheduled tasks (ONLOGON trigger, HIGHEST privilege) + registry Run keys • C2 uses PBKDF2-SHA1 key derivation, AES-256-CBC + HMAC-SHA256 authentication
DFIR artifacts include MLPCInstallHelper.exe in %TEMP%, SVCData\Config directory, HostDataProcess scheduled task, and MTXCNBV11000ERCXSWOLZNBVRGH mutex. Hunt for unsigned .
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039055908194406508

item.title: Compromised axios npm package delivers cross-platform RAT
item.description:
Supply chain attack: Compromised axios npm maintainer account delivers cross-platform RAT to 3M+ weekly downloads. 3-hour window on March 31st affected two malicious releases before npm intervention.
Key technical details: • Attacker hijacked jasonsaayman account, published axios@1.14.1 & 0.30.4 with typosquat dependency "plain-crypto-js" • Postinstall script downloads platform-specific RATs from sfrclak[.]com:8000/6202033 (T1195.002) • Windows: Persistence via HKCU Run key "MicrosoftUpdate" + %PROGRAMDATA%\system.bat • macOS: Binary masquerades as /Library/Cache/com.apple.act.mond Apple daemon • Linux: Python RAT crashes in containers due to os.getlogin() bug
Attack bypassed GitHub Actions OIDC by using compromised account directly. Registry metadata shows publisher shift from "GitHub Actions" to direct user session and email change from jasonsaayman@gmail.com to ifstap@proton.me.
RAT capabilities include directory enumeration, shell execution, and binary injection. All variants beacon every 60 seconds with system info to C2.
Hunt for outbound connections to 142.11.206.73:8000 and file artifacts: wt.exe, system.bat, com.apple.act.mond, ld.py. Check npm registry metadata for trusted publisher gaps.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039040835518599289

item.title: Inside the Axios supply chain compromise - one RAT to rule them all
item.description:
Massive supply chain compromise: Axios npm package backdoored with cross-platform RAT reaching ~100 million weekly downloads. Compromised maintainer account deployed identical C2 protocol across Windows, macOS, and Linux systems.
Campaign details: • Compromised jasonsaayman maintainer account published axios@1.14.1 (latest) and axios@0.30.4 (legacy) with malicious plain-crypto-js dependency • Postinstall hook automatically downloads platform-specific RAT implants from sfrclak[.]com:8000 (T1195.002) • Stage-2 payloads: PowerShell (Windows), C++ (macOS), Python (Linux) - all implementing identical C2 protocol and command set • Anti-forensic cleanup: dropper self-deletes and swaps package.json to remove postinstall evidence (T1070.004) • Attribution overlap with WAVESHAPER/UNC1069 (DPRK-linked) based on macOS binary analysis
Key artifacts: • Hardcoded User-Agent: "mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0; windows nt 5.1; trident/4.0)" - anomalous on macOS/Linux • C2 communications: Base64-encoded JSON over HTTP POST every 60 seconds • Windows persistence: Registry Run key + hidden batch file • Command types: kill, peinject, runscript, rundir with platform-native execution
Hunt for IE8/Windows XP user-agent strings from macOS/Linux hosts and npm packages with recent postinstall hooks. Full IOCs and detection rules available from Elastic Security Labs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039025796812361733

item.title: WhatsApp malware campaign delivers VBS payloads and MSI backdoors
item.description:
Microsoft Security tracks sophisticated WhatsApp malware campaign using VBS scripts and cloud hosting to deploy MSI backdoors. Multi-stage infection chain combines social engineering with renamed Windows utilities and UAC bypass techniques.
Key technical details: • Initial access via WhatsApp-delivered VBS files creating hidden folders in C:\ProgramData, dropping renamed curl.exe/bitsadmin.exe as netapi.dll/sc.exe • Stage 2 retrieves secondary payloads (auxs.vbs, WinUpdate_KB5034231.vbs) from AWS S3, Tencent Cloud, and Backblaze B2 • UAC bypass attempts modify HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Win registry entries, continuously retry cmd.exe elevation • Final payload: Unsigned MSI installers (Setup.msi, WinRAR.msi, AnyDesk.msi) enabling persistent remote access • PE metadata discrepancy detection: Renamed binaries retain original OriginalFileName field (curl.exe/bitsadmin.exe)
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor ProcessCommandLine with ProgramData + downloader flags (-K, -s, -L, -o, https:) • Hunt renamed executables where InitiatingProcessVersionInfoOriginalFileName != actual filename • Registry changes under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Win + ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin modifications
Detection opportunity: Flag files where PE OriginalFileName metadata doesn't match actual filename - attackers retain original metadata when renaming legitimate tools.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2039010625415004491

item.title: Elastic releases detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
item.description:
Elastic releases comprehensive detection rules for active Axios supply chain attack affecting versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Cross-platform compromise uses transitive dependency for post-install execution, deploying platform-specific RATs.
Technical breakdown: • Malicious packages: axios 1.14.1/0.30.4 + plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 dependency trigger post-install execution • Attack pattern: node → OS shell (sh/cscript/osascript) → remote payload fetch → backgrounded execution • Linux: Python RAT polls C2 every 60s, enumerates /proc, supports runscript/peinject commands • Windows: Renamed PowerShell proxy (wt.exe), registry persistence via Run\MicrosoftUpdate key, base64 beacon • macOS: AppleScript → fake Apple binary (com.apple.act.mond), universal Mach-O with debug symbols intact
Key IOCs: • C2: sfrclak[.]com:8000 (142.11.206[.]73) • File paths: /tmp/ld.py, %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond • Fake User-Agent: mozilla/4.0 (compatible; msie 8.0; windows nt 5.1; trident/4.0)
Hunt for Node.js processes spawning shells with HTTP download commands immediately after npm installs. Full detection rules and IOCs available in Elastic's release.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038995676013695060

item.title: Operation TrueChaos: 0-Day Exploitation Against Southeast Asian Government Targets
item.description:
Active zero-day exploitation: Chinese 🇨🇳-nexus threat actors weaponize TrueConf video conferencing update mechanism to deploy Havoc payloads against Southeast Asian government networks. CVE-2026-3502 (CVSS 7.8) exploited in Operation TrueChaos campaign.
Technical breakdown: • CVE-2026-3502: TrueConf client lacks integrity validation in update process, allowing arbitrary code execution via compromised on-premises server • Attack chain: Malicious trueconf_windows_update.exe → drops poweriso.exe + 7z-x64.dll → DLL sideloading → UAC bypass via iscsicpl.exe hijacking iscsiexe.dll • C2 infrastructure on Alibaba/Tencent cloud (43.134.90[.]60, 43.134.52[.]221, 47.237.15[.]197) serving Havoc framework • Persistence via HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\UpdateCheck registry key • Overlapping ShadowPad activity suggests coordinated Chinese APT operations
Hunt for unsigned trueconf_windows_update.exe, poweriso.exe in C:\ProgramData\PowerISO\, and process chain trueconf.exe → trueconf_windows_update.exe spawning cmd.exe with curl/winrar commands.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038980427889905997

item.title: Axios Supply Chain Attack: Analysis & Incident Response
item.description:
Active supply chain compromise: Axios npm package trojanized with cross-platform RAT targeting developer environments and CI/CD pipelines. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 published March 30-31, 2026 using compromised maintainer account.
Key technical details: • RAT deploys across Windows, macOS, Linux with 60-second C2 beaconing via base64-encoded JSON over HTTP POST • Windows persistence via registry run key "MicrosoftUpdate" pointing to %PROGRAMDATA%\system.bat • Memory-only .NET reflective loading on Windows evades disk-based detection • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak[.]com and callnrwise[.]com, created <24hrs before attack • Possible overlap with DPRK-nexus WAVESHAPER malware family (UNC1069)
Attack methodology: • Compromised lead maintainer npm account to publish trojanized versions • Introduces dependency "plain-crypto-js" as delivery mechanism • Enumerates Documents, Desktop, config paths for high-value data targeting • Legacy User-Agent: mozilla/4.0 suggests evasion tactics
DFIR artifacts: • Windows: %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe, system.bat, registry persistence under HKCU Run keys • macOS: /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond, /tmp/.XXXXXX.scpt • Linux: /tmp/ld.py • Network: Outbound HTTP POST to C2 domains with base64 JSON payloads
Immediate actions: Check package-lock.json for axios@1.14.1/0.30.4, downgrade to 1.40.0, rotate all exposed secrets/tokens, hunt for persistence mechanisms and C2 communication patterns.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038965444036403318

item.title: Common Entra ID Security Assessment Findings – Part 2: Privileged Unprotected Groups
item.description:
Compass Security identifies critical privilege escalation risks in Entra ID environments where unprotected groups are used for sensitive access controls and Azure role assignments.
Key findings: • Unprotected groups lack role-assignable properties, RMAU membership, or on-premises sync - allowing lower-tier admins (Groups Administrator, User Administrator, etc.) to modify membership • Issue 1: Conditional Access policies using unprotected groups enable MFA bypass through group membership manipulation • Issue 2: PIM for Groups allows non-role-assignable groups as eligible members, creating escalation paths to Global Administrator • Issue 3: Azure role assignments via unprotected groups can grant cloud-to-on-premises privilege escalation through Azure Arc command execution
Attack chain example: • Compromised service principal with Group.ReadWrite.All adds itself to unprotected group • Group has Owner role on Azure subscription containing Arc-connected domain controller • Attacker executes commands as SYSTEM on DC via New-AzConnectedMachineRunCommand • Results in full Active Directory compromise from cloud identity
Use EntraFalcon tool to identify Finding ID GRP-005 for privileged unprotected groups. Remediate by placing sensitive groups in Restricted Management Administrative Units or converting to role-assignable groups.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038935072280068473

item.title: Axios npm Hijack 2026: Everything You Need to Know – IOCs, Impact & Remediation
item.description:
Major supply chain attack: Axios npm package (~100M weekly downloads) compromised for 2-3 hours on March 31, 2026. Malicious versions deployed cross-platform RAT via postinstall script, bypassing CI/CD controls through hijacked maintainer account.
Key technical details: • Malicious versions: axios@1.14.1, axios@0.30.4, plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 (SHA1: 2553649f232204966871cea80a5d0d6adc700ca, d6f3f62fd3b9f5432f5782b62d8cfd5247d5ee71, 07d889e2dadce6f3910dcbc253317d28ca61c766) • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak.com (142.11.206.73:8000) with OS-specific beacon endpoints (/product0-2) • Attack bypassed GitHub Actions by using stolen npm token - no OIDC provenance metadata present • Self-deleting RAT deployed to %PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe (Windows), /Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond (macOS), /tmp/ld.py (Linux)
Attack methodology: • Compromised jasonsaayman's npm account, changed email to ifstap@proton.me • Pre-staged decoy package 18 hours before main attack for credibility • Hidden dependency plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 contained obfuscated setup.js (XOR key OrDeR_7077) • Targeted both latest and legacy tags for maximum reach
DFIR artifacts: • Check lockfiles for affected versions and plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 presence • Hunt for filesystem IOCs and outbound connections to sfrclak.com since March 30 • Missing OIDC provenance on npm packages should trigger alerts • Search DNS logs and EDR for C2 communication patterns

item.title: Axios Supply Chain Attack Exposes Developers to Hidden Malware
item.description:
Axios npm supply chain attack deploys cross-platform RAT through compromised maintainer account. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 used phantom dependency "plain-crypto-js" to bypass detection and target 300M+ weekly users.
Key technical details: • Attacker hijacked core maintainer npm account, changed email to ProtonMail, bypassed GitHub Actions CI/CD • Injected fake dependency plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 with post-install script contacting C2 at sfrclak.com:8000/6202033 • Cross-platform payloads: macOS (AppleScript + /Library/Caches), Windows (VBScript/PowerShell), Linux (Python /tmp/ld.py) • Anti-forensics: XOR/base64 obfuscation, self-deletion after 36 seconds, replaced package.json with clean decoy • Attack window: ~3 hours for malicious Axios versions, 4+ hours for poisoned dependency
Attack methodology: • Manual npm publish bypassed trusted publisher metadata and OIDC authentication (T1195.002) • Phantom dependency never referenced in source code but auto-executed during npm install • C2 traffic disguised as legitimate npm registry activity to evade network detection • Platform-specific RAT variants with tailored persistence mechanisms
Hunt for npm packages with manual publishing records lacking GitHub Actions metadata, unusual dependency additions in recent versions, and outbound connections to low-reputation domains during package installation.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038904758485033043

item.title: The Axios npm Compromise: How the Internet’s Most Popular HTTP Client Became a Trojan Horse
item.description:
Axios npm package compromised via hijacked maintainer account, delivering cross-platform RAT to 300M+ weekly downloads. Malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 use phantom dependency and self-erasing payload to evade detection.
Technical breakdown: • Attackers compromised jasonsaayman maintainer account, bypassed GitHub OIDC by generating classic npm token • Phantom dependency plain-crypto-js@4.2.1 triggers postinstall hook without code import requirement • Cross-platform RAT targets macOS (/Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond), Windows (disguised PowerShell as wt.exe), Linux (/tmp/ld.py) • C2 infrastructure: sfrclak.com:8000 for payload delivery and command execution • Self-destruct mechanism deletes setup.js, replaces package.json with clean version, orphans process to PID 1
DFIR artifacts: • npm registry metadata shows missing OIDC binding for malicious releases • RAT persistence files at system cache locations mimicking legitimate processes • Postinstall execution detaches from npm process tree for forensic evasion
Check for compromise: npm list axios | grep -E "1\.14\.1|0\.30\.4" and hunt for RAT artifacts at documented file paths. Downgrade immediately to 1.14.0/0.30.3.

item.title: Tracking TeamPCP: Investigating Post-Compromise Attacks Seen in the Wild | Wiz Blog
item.description:
TeamPCP threat actors rapidly weaponize stolen credentials from recent supply chain attacks against Trivy, KICS, LiteLLM, and Telnyx. Post-compromise activity observed within hours, targeting cloud environments and repositories for mass data exfiltration.
Key technical details: • Supply chain compromises March 19-27: Trivy, KICS, LiteLLM, Telnyx packages injected with credential-harvesting malware • TruffleHog used for rapid secret validation via sts:GetCallerIdentity API calls within hours of theft • AWS discovery operations: ListUsers, DescribeInstances, ListSecrets targeting IAM, EC2, RDS, S3, ECS environments • GitHub abuse via stolen PATs: malicious workflows in pull requests, bulk git.clone operations, workflow log deletion • ECS Exec + SSM Agent for container command execution and data exfiltration • Infrastructure: Mullvad VPN exit nodes, InterServer hosting, bold naming conventions ("pawn", "massive-exfil")
Post-compromise artifacts: • Anomalous GetCallerIdentity, ListSecrets, GetSecretValue API patterns • Mass git.clone events and workflow log deletions • ExecuteCommand via SSM on containers not in original images • VPN/ASO anomalies in cloud authentication logs
Hunt for unusual enumeration patterns, VPN-sourced API calls, and bulk secret access events. Full IOC list includes 11 IP addresses and specific User-Agent strings.

item.title: Detecting an email-based ClickFix attack that delivers DCRat malware payload · Blog · Sublime Security
item.description:
ClickFix campaign delivers DCRat via fake Booking.com rental emails and JavaScript clipboard manipulation. Attackers use social engineering with fabricated apartment rental threads to trick users into executing PowerShell payloads.
Attack chain breakdown: • Initial vector: Fake email thread about apartment rental handoff due to colleague's illness • Malicious link leads to real Cloudflare CAPTCHA followed by fake CAPTCHA page mimicking Booking.com • JavaScript automatically copies obfuscated PowerShell to clipboard when "CAPTCHA" clicked • Social engineering instructions: "Press Windows+R, then CTRL+V and ENTER" to execute payload • PowerShell downloads DCRat from gettsveriff[.]com, saves as C:\Windows\Temp\tybd7.exe
DCRat C2 infrastructure: • Primary servers: hkfasfsafg[.]click, hfjwfheiwf[.]click, jfhaowhfjk[.]click • Capabilities: Shell commands, keylogging, file exfiltration, credential harvesting • SHA256: 08037de4a729634fa818ddf03ddd27c28c89f42158af5ede71cf0ede71cf0ae2d78fa198
Hunt for recent PowerShell executions downloading to %TEMP% and outbound connections to newly registered domains with random character patterns. Full IOC list available in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038663102653145543

item.title: TeamPCP’s Telnyx Attack Marks a Shift in Tactics Beyond LiteLLM
item.description:
TeamPCP evolves beyond LiteLLM with Telnyx PyPI compromise, introducing WAV steganography and Windows persistence. Attack exposed 700K+ downloads for 6.5 hours before PyPI quarantine.
Technical evolution from LiteLLM: • Split-file injection across 3 locations in _client.py evades visual inspection • WAV steganography hides credential harvester in audio files (hangup.wav, ringtone.wav) • Windows support adds %APPDATA%\Startup\msbuild.exe persistence with CREATE_NO_WINDOW flag • Infrastructure shift from HTTPS domains to plaintext HTTP 83[.]142[.]209[.]203:8080 • Anti-replay mechanism via msbuild.exe.lock with 12-hour cooldown
Attack chain: • Malicious Telnyx 4.87.1/4.87.2 published to PyPI without GitHub commits • Import triggers Base64 decode wrapper _d() and platform detection • Downloads WAV files containing XOR-encrypted payloads (8-byte rotating key) • Establishes persistence and exfiltrates via tpcp.tar.gz HTTP POST
DFIR artifacts: • WAV downloads from non-media IPs over port 8080 • Unexpected msbuild.exe in user Startup folders • X-Filename: tpcp.tar.gz headers in HTTP traffic • Hidden .lock files via attrib +h commands
Hunt for WAV file downloads to port 8080 and msbuild.exe processes with suppressed windows. Full IOCs and hunting queries available in Trend Micro report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038648041868284363

item.title: Professional Networks Under Attack: Vietnam-Linked Actors Deploy PXA Stealer in Global Infostealer Campaign
item.description:
Vietnamese 🇻🇳 cybercriminals weaponize LinkedIn job recruitment to deploy PXA Stealer across India 🇮🇳, Bangladesh 🇧🇩, Netherlands 🇳🇱, Sweden 🇸🇪, and US 🇺🇸. Campaign uses compromised professional accounts for lateral spread, creating exponential infection potential through trusted connections.
Technical breakdown: • Delivery chain: LinkedIn DM → Google Forms → Dropbox ZIP → DLL sideloading via legitimate winword.exe (T1574.002) • Evasion: 100MB artificially inflated DLL bypasses size-threshold scanners; in-memory Python execution via exec() leaves minimal disk artifacts • C2: Dynamic infrastructure retrieved from encrypted Telegram channel; final payload hosted on IP masquerading as Chinese 🇨🇳 gov infrastructure • Targets: Browser credentials, crypto wallets (MetaMask, Coinbase), 2FA tokens, hardware wallet artifacts, email clients • Persistence: Scheduled task mimics Microsoft Edge update process in %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\WindowsApps
Hunt for oversized DLLs (>50MB) loaded by Office processes, Python executables in Edge directories, and scheduled tasks with Edge-related names but non-standard paths.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038633176738730411

item.title: Keitaro TDS abused to deliver AutoIT-based loader targeting German speakers · Blog · Sublime Security
item.description:
German 🇩🇪-targeted romance scam campaign leverages Keitaro TDS for geo-filtering and delivers 300MB AutoIT-based loader via password-protected ISO files. Campaign uses explicit content lures with sophisticated evasion techniques including AV detection and scheduled task persistence.
Technical breakdown: • Initial vector: Romance-themed emails with malicious links to tradesurp[.]com and privatephotos[.]online domains • Keitaro TDS performs geo-filtering (German locales only) + delivers oversized ISO (300MB) from Russia 🇷🇺-based infrastructure • ISO contains AutoIT interpreter + heavily obfuscated 11,500-line script (SHA256: 551f05395664da2e8385366b95266af9bb3f953d022e737f3defecf7f0289bc4) • Batch script (Burden.mil.bat) detects AV services (bdservicehost, SophosHealth, AvastUI) and modifies execution flow accordingly • Persistence via scheduled task "DragonMapper" in %LocalAppData%\Ecovision Solutions\ (T1053.005) • Keitaro TDS identified by Base64 cookie "3f06b" - key indicator for this platform
Hunt for scheduled tasks containing "DragonMapper", oversized ISO files in Downloads, and AutoIT3.exe processes spawned from %LocalAppData%. Full IOC list includes file hashes and network indicators.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038617850340917450

item.title: AITM phishing with Russian infrastructure and detection evasion from a lapsed domain · Blog · Sublime Security
item.description:
Russian 🇷🇺-hosted AITM phishing campaign exploits expired US 🇺🇸 law firm domain to bypass detection. Multi-stage attack uses sophisticated browser fingerprinting and WebSocket control channels to steal Microsoft credentials while maintaining persistent access.
Technical breakdown: • Initial vector: Teams meeting invite from compromised dilloncriminallaw[.]com (expired 2023, re-registered via Chinese 🇨🇳 GNAME registrar) • Gate page at meeting.sa[.]com performs 4-layer evasion: automated analysis detection, domain blocking (targets "belfius", "baringa"), user-agent analysis, plugin verification • Stage 1: 450KB obfuscated JavaScript creates WebSocket connections, runs embedded Rust/WebAssembly code for advanced fingerprinting • Stage 2: Server-side validation redirects to randomized subdomain at team.za[.]com with hardcoded folder "6802a801d7f11fb0bef7f792" • Final stage: Branded Office 365 login harvests credentials via legitimate Microsoft authentication proxy
Campaign uses custom encoding (substitution cipher → hex → Base32 → Base64) to decode victim emails from URL paths. WebSocket endpoint wss://52e42e6675254286a4273c62f3b19bfb.team.za[.]com enables real-time attack orchestration.
Hunt for Teams invitations from non-corporate domains with excessive invisible characters and monitor WebSocket connections to .za/.ru domains with hex-encoded folder paths.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038602797227053227

item.title: Threats based on Clipboards actions (+ KQL Query)
item.description:
New analysis reveals clipboard-based data theft techniques using PowerShell cmdlets to silently harvest sensitive information from compromised endpoints. Simple .cmd scripts can run for days collecting passwords, tokens, and other copied data.
Key technical details: • Threat actors abuse Get-Clipboard, Set-Clipboard PowerShell cmdlets for persistent data collection (T1115) • Malicious scripts log all Ctrl+C activity to text files: Add-Content -Path 'clipboard_log.txt' -Value "[$timestamp] $current" • Set-Clipboard -AsOSC52 enables remote clipboard injection over SSH/RDP sessions • Windows Clipboard History (Win+V) stores multiple items accessible via GetClipboardData APIs • Registry keys and cbdhsvc.exe process activity indicate clipboard monitoring
Attack methodology: • Deploy .cmd wrapper executing PowerShell clipboard monitoring loop • Script runs silently collecting copied passwords, cryptocurrency addresses, tokens • Data exfiltration triggered after sufficient information gathered • Persistence through background execution, minimal system footprint
DFIR artifacts: • ProcessCommandLine events containing "clipboard" + "powershell.exe" • Registry modifications enabling Clipboard History feature • cbdhsvc.exe and svchost.exe API call patterns in EDR logs • Clipboard_log.txt or similar output files in temp directories
Use this KQL query for detection: DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessCommandLine contains "clipboard" | where FileName has "powershell.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038542312947519824

item.title: Resoker: A Telegram Based Remote Access Trojan
item.description:
K7 Labs dissects ResokerRAT, a new Telegram-controlled Remote Access Trojan that bypasses traditional C2 detection by leveraging the Telegram Bot API for command execution and data exfiltration.
Technical breakdown: • Uses mutex "Global\ResokerSystemMutex" for single-instance enforcement and anti-debugging via IsDebuggerPresent API • Implements keyboard hooking (T1056.001) to block critical key combinations: Alt+Tab, Ctrl+Shift+Esc, Ctrl+Alt+Del, Windows key • Establishes persistence through HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run registry modification (T1547.001) • Executes commands via hidden PowerShell including /screenshot, /download, /block_taskmgr, and UAC manipulation • Terminates analysis tools: Taskmgr.exe, Procexp.exe, ProcessHacker.exe using TerminateProcess API
C2 communication uses hardcoded Telegram bot token (8679995457:AAHiI3UDWmJaj4etgI4fAZK_wo4KJfhXLWc) with chat ID 8558596408. Hunt for outbound HTTPS to api.telegram.org with suspicious bot tokens and monitor for PowerShell execution with -WindowStyle Hidden parameter.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038527203655463276

item.title: Please, We Beg, Just One Weekend Free Of Appliances (Citrix NetScaler CVE-2026-3055 Memory Overread Part 2)
item.description:
CVE-2026-3055 reveals multiple memory overread bugs in Citrix NetScaler affecting /saml/login and /wsfed/passive endpoints. In-the-wild exploitation confirmed since March 27th, 2026.
Technical details: • CVE-2026-3055 encompasses at least TWO distinct memory overread vulnerabilities • Second variant targets GET /wsfed/passive?wctx (missing = symbol triggers buffer overrun) • Leaked memory appears base64-encoded in NSC_TASS cookie responses • Only exploitable when NetScaler configured as SAML Identity Provider • Memory disclosure reveals HTTP headers, session IDs, and administrative tokens
Attack methodology: • Simple GET request to /wsfed/passive?wctx (no value, no equals sign) • Vulnerable appliances return kilobytes of leaked memory content • Multiple requests yield different memory chunks each time • Administrative session hijacking possible via leaked session cookies
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor for requests to /wsfed/passive?wctx and /saml/login endpoints • Large NSC_TASS cookie values (base64-encoded leaked memory) • 302 redirects with abnormally long Set-Cookie headers • Citrix-ns-orig-srcip headers in leaked memory indicate internal requests
Hunt for GET requests to these endpoints without proper query parameters. Detection script and IOCs available in watchTowr Labs report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038391296839004222

item.title: iOS Lockdown mode and forensic analysis: a technical perspective
item.description:
Apple's iOS Lockdown Mode creates systematic barriers to mobile forensic acquisition by blocking wired connections on locked devices, restricting pairing workflows, and altering artifact creation patterns across messaging, web browsing, and media sharing.
Technical impact breakdown: • Wired acquisition paths disrupted: requires device unlock before USB connection, blocking standard logical, advanced logical (AFC), and agent-based extraction on locked devices • Pairing record workflows compromised: existing trust relationships may be refused until authentication, while enabling/disabling mode requires restart that destroys AFU state • Evidence profile altered: months in Lockdown Mode suppress link previews, Shared Albums, complex JavaScript artifacts, and FaceTime metadata from unknown contacts • Commercial tool limitations: FBI CART failed extraction on reporter's Lockdown Mode iPhone (Feb 2026 case), highlighting real-world bypass challenges • Hardware generation matters: checkm8 bootrom exploit (iPhone X/A11 and earlier) operates below iOS level and remains unaffected
Document Lockdown Mode presence at triage alongside lock state and iOS version. Prioritize alternative evidence sources: synchronized hosts, iCloud backups, and application cloud storage when direct extraction is blocked.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038376194949648873

item.title: An open-source forensic exporter for ChatGPT conversations (SHA-256 hashing, verification, full project enumeration)
item.description:
New open-source tool enables forensic collection of complete ChatGPT conversation histories with cryptographic verification and structured export capabilities. Addresses key challenges in AI chat platform evidence preservation.
Technical features: • Chrome DevTools Protocol integration extracts auth tokens, bypasses UI limitations • SHA-256 hashing with CSV manifest tracking ensures chain of custody integrity • Multi-phase discovery process handles hidden projects beyond sidebar API (only shows ~5 pinned) • Tree structure parsing captures conversation branches, edits, and regenerations • Team/Enterprise workspace support requires separate account ID headers
Collection challenges solved: • Rate limiting mitigation through exponential backoff and automatic retry logic • Resume functionality for interrupted collections maintains forensic continuity • PDF conversion scripts generate court-ready formatted outputs • Post-collection verification script detects hash mismatches and missing artifacts
DFIR practitioners can leverage this for AI-assisted investigation cases, IP theft involving AI tools, and employee misconduct investigations. Tool provides faster, more complete extraction than OpenAI's native 24-48hr export delays.
Available on GitHub at chatgpt-forensic-exporter. Consider for ChatGPT evidence collection workflows and personal data backup scenarios.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2038119398448439584

item.title: The Sequels Are Never As Good, But We're Still In Pain (Citrix NetScaler CVE-2026-3055 Memory Overread)
item.description:
Watchtowr Labs analyzes CVE-2026-3055, another Citrix NetScaler memory overread vulnerability affecting appliances configured as SAML IdPs. CVSS 9.3 flaw echoes previous CitrixBleed incidents with session data disclosure potential.
Technical breakdown: • CVE-2026-3055 affects NetScaler ADC/Gateway versions before 14.1-26.x when configured as SAML Identity Provider • Vulnerability triggered by sending minimal SAML AuthnRequest without AssertionConsumerServiceURL parameter • Memory disclosure occurs in NSC_TASS cookie containing fragments like 0xdeadbeef markers and previous request data • Exploitation leaves verbose logs in /var/log/ns.log with malformed ProtocolBinding entries • Patched versions return "Parsing of presented Assertion failed" error instead of cookies
Attack vector requires POST to /saml/login endpoint with crafted base64-encoded XML payload missing specific SAML parameters. Memory disclosure limited to ~100 bytes per attempt, terminated by NULL bytes.
Detection opportunity: Monitor for NSC_TASS cookies issued after malformed SAML requests and unusual /var/log/ns.log entries with truncated ProtocolBinding values.
Hunt for appliances returning cookies instead of parsing errors when processing minimal SAML requests. Watchtowr provides detection payload in their analysis.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2037998644322783310
